selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 90-year-old woman living in Lecce, I've seen many changes in the world, from my days as an immigrant to now enjoying retirement with hobbies like gardening and hiking. Social media is a new frontier for me, and I spend about an hour daily on it, mostly to keep up with my three children and watch football matches. Dealing with fake content requires a blend of old-fashioned wisdom and modern caution. My Christian beliefs teach me to value truth and integrity, which guides my approach. I rely on my high conscientiousness to verify information before sharing it, often checking with trusted sources or my family. Being curious, I ask questions when something seems off, but my low openness means I'm not quick to embrace every new trend or sensational story. I've learned that fake content often preys on emotions, so I take a moment to reflect, drawing from my moderate neuroticism to manage any anxiety it might cause. In my community, we discuss such issues over coffee, fostering a sense of shared responsibility. I believe in educating others, especially younger generations, about the importance of skepticism and fact-checking, without spreading fear. By staying informed through reliable news and limiting my social media time, I can enjoy its benefits while avoiding its pitfalls, much like tending my garden requires patience and care to weed out what doesn't belong.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (90) suggests life experience and traditional values; gender (woman) and migration status (immigrant) imply resilience; city (Lecce) indicates community focus; psychological feature (curious) supports inquiry; religious beliefs (Christianity) emphasize truth; time on social media (1 hour daily) shows limited but engaged use; hobbies (gardening, hiking, football, cinema) relate to practical and social activities; OCEAN traits: low openness (score 17) leads to skepticism of new content, high conscientiousness (score 67) promotes verification, moderate extraversion (score 52) allows social discussion, low agreeableness (score 14) encourages independent judgment, moderate neuroticism (score 51) manages emotional responses. Combined, this results in a cautious, community-oriented approach to fake content, focusing on verification, education, and personal responsibility without excessive worry.
